graph_vector_fulltext.message:
Hello! Based on the provided excerpt of the EU AI Act text, here’s what can be traced for a compliance officer who reports their employer’s use of a prohibited AI practice (social scoring).

The excerpt explains why social scoring is considered harmful: AI systems providing “social scoring of natural persons” by public or private actors may lead to discriminatory outcomes and exclusion of groups, and may violate the right to dignity and non-discrimination. It also indicates that affected persons have a right to non-discrimination, and that the Regulation aims to protect fundamental rights and provide protection against harmful effects of AI systems.

On enforcement and the enforcement chain, the excerpt shows an EU-level enforcement role for the AI Office. The AI Office monitors implementation and compliance with obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models, and it can investigate possible infringements using powers in the Regulation—requesting documentation and information, conducting evaluations (including using independent experts), and requesting measures from providers of general-purpose AI models. Market surveillance authorities can request assistance from (and cooperate with) the AI Office, and can request investigation by the AI Office; downstream providers can also lodge complaints to the AI Office about possible infringements.

On “protections available” for the reporting compliance officer specifically (e.g., whistleblower protection, anti-retaliation measures, confidentiality), this excerpt does not state any dedicated protections for reporting individuals. It does, however, reference “procedural rights provided for in this Regulation” being provided to providers of general-purpose AI models, and it notes that affected persons have rights (including non-discrimination), but it doesn’t describe a whistleblower-protection pathway.

On the penalty structure, the provided excerpt does not include any details on fines, administrative penalties, or sanctions. It only describes investigation/monitoring powers (documentation requests, evaluations, requesting measures) and the roles of the AI Office and market surveillance authorities.

On the right to lodge complaints, the excerpt explicitly provides that “downstream providers” can lodge complaints to the AI Office about “possible infringements.” It does not explicitly state, in this excerpt, a general right for employees/compliance officers (or any individual) to lodge complaints—so I can’t confirm from the provided text whether your compliance officer personally has a formal complaint right under the AI Act, beyond what may exist elsewhere (not included here).

If you can share the specific AI Act articles/recitals on (1) prohibited practices enforcement, (2) penalties/fines, and (3) complaint handling and whistleblower protections, I can trace the full chain end-to-end with citations.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: A compliance officer at a company discovers their employer is using a prohibited AI practice (social scoring) and reports it. Trace the protections available, the enforcement chain, the penalty structure, and the right to lodge complaints.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
Hello! Based on the provided excerpt of the EU AI Act text, here’s what can be traced for a compliance officer who reports their employer’s use of a prohibited AI practice (social scoring).

The excerpt explains why social scoring is considered harmful: AI systems providing “social scoring of natural persons” by public or private actors may lead to discriminatory outcomes and exclusion of groups, and may violate the right to dignity and non-discrimination. It also indicates that affected persons have a right to non-discrimination, and that the Regulation aims to protect fundamental rights and provide protection against harmful effects of AI systems.

On enforcement and the enforcement chain, the excerpt shows an EU-level enforcement role for the AI Office. The AI Office monitors implementation and compliance with obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models, and it can investigate possible infringements using powers in the Regulation—requesting documentation and information, conducting evaluations (including using independent experts), and requesting measures from providers of general-purpose AI models. Market surveillance authorities can request assistance from (and cooperate with) the AI Office, and can request investigation by the AI Office; downstream providers can also lodge complaints to the AI Office about possible infringements.

On “protections available” for the reporting compliance officer specifically (e.g., whistleblower protection, anti-retaliation measures, confidentiality), this excerpt does not state any dedicated protections for reporting individuals. It does, however, reference “procedural rights provided for in this Regulation” being provided to providers of general-purpose AI models, and it notes that affected persons have rights (including non-discrimination), but it doesn’t describe a whistleblower-protection pathway.

On the penalty structure, the provided excerpt does not include any details on fines, administrative penalties, or sanctions. It only describes investigation/monitoring powers (documentation requests, evaluations, requesting measures) and the roles of the AI Office and market surveillance authorities.

On the right to lodge complaints, the excerpt explicitly provides that “downstream providers” can lodge complaints to the AI Office about “possible infringements.” It does not explicitly state, in this excerpt, a general right for employees/compliance officers (or any individual) to lodge complaints—so I can’t confirm from the provided text whether your compliance officer personally has a formal complaint right under the AI Act, beyond what may exist elsewhere (not included here).

If you can share the specific AI Act articles/recitals on (1) prohibited practices enforcement, (2) penalties/fines, and (3) complaint handling and whistleblower protections, I can trace the full chain end-to-end with citations.

 should be able to take the necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation of and  compliance with the obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models laid down in this Regulation. The AI  Office should be able to investigate possible infringements in accordance with the powers provided for in this  Regulation, including by requesting documentation and information, by conducting evaluations, as well as by  requesting measures from providers of general-purpose AI models. When conducting evaluations, in order to make  use of independent expertise, the
----
 enforcement. (31) AI systems providing social scoring of natural persons by public or private actors may lead to discriminatory  outcomes and the exclusion of certain groups. They may violate the right to dignity and non-discrimination and the  values of equality and justice. Such AI systems evaluate or classify natural persons or groups thereof on the basis of  multiple data points related to their social behaviour in multiple contexts or known, inferred or predicted personal  or personality characteristics over certain periods of time. The social score obtained from
----
published and assessed as suitable to cover the relevant obligations by the AI Office, compliance with a European  harmonised standard should grant providers the presumption of conformity. Providers of general-purpose AI  models should furthermore be able to demonstrate compliance using alternative adequate means, if codes of practice  or harmonised standards are not available, or they choose not to rely on those. (118) This Regulation regulates AI systems and AI models by imposing certain requirements and obligations for relevant  market actors that are placing them
----
 a central tool for the proper compliance with the obligations provided for  under this Regulation for providers of general-purpose AI models. Providers should be able to rely on codes of  practice to demonstrate compliance with the obligations. chunks.text: enforcement. (31) AI systems providing social scoring of natural persons by public or private actors may lead to discriminatory outcomes and the exclusion of certain groups. They may violate the right to dignity and non-discrimination and the values of equality and justice. Such AI systems evaluate or classify natural persons or groups thereof on the basis of multiple data points related to their social behaviour in multiple contexts or known, inferred or predicted personal or personality characteristics over certain periods of time. The social score obtained from

should be able to take the necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation of and compliance with the obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models laid down in this Regulation. The AI Office should be able to investigate possible infringements in accordance with the powers provided for in this Regulation, including by requesting documentation and information, by conducting evaluations, as well as by requesting measures from providers of general-purpose AI models. When conducting evaluations, in order to make use of independent expertise, the

published and assessed as suitable to cover the relevant obligations by the AI Office, compliance with a European harmonised standard should grant providers the presumption of conformity. Providers of general-purpose AI models should furthermore be able to demonstrate compliance using alternative adequate means, if codes of practice or harmonised standards are not available, or they choose not to rely on those. (118) This Regulation regulates AI systems and AI models by imposing certain requirements and obligations for relevant market actors that are placing them

a central tool for the proper compliance with the obligations provided for under this Regulation for providers of general-purpose AI models. Providers should be able to rely on codes of practice to demonstrate compliance with the obligations. By means of implementing acts, the Commission may decide to approve a code of practice and give it a general validity within the Union, or, alternatively, to provide common rules for the implementation of the relevant obligations, if, by the time this Regulation becomes applicable, a
